retrieved_docs.title: Bone Metastasis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Carcinoma is the most common cause of secondary bone cancer. Many bone metastases originate from disseminated breast, lung, and prostate cancer. Although, increasingly renal cell and thyroid cancer can metastasize to bone. Carcinomas are of epithelial tissue origin and go on to form the covering over solid organs and glands throughout the body. Non-epithelial cell lines produce cancers that spread to the bone. Hematologic malignancies such as multiple myeloma and lymphoma involve bone. Similarly, mesenchymal tumors such as sarcomas, can exhibit late metastases to the bone as well. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Scan -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The sensitivity of bone scan for metastatic disease varies for each primary tumor. The specifics of sensitivity and specificity for each type of malignancy is beyond the scope of this article; however, the study is most accurate in the setting of osteoblastic metastases and least accurate with malignancies that typically produce osteolytic bone lesions. Given such, the common malignancies for which bone scintigraphy is known to have a high sensitivity for identifying bone metastases include breast, prostate, and lung cancer. The common malignancies for which bone scintigraphy is known to have a low sensitivity for detection of osseous metastases include multiple myeloma, renal cell carcinoma, and thyroid carcinoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis in the lung is a complex multistep process. Metastatic tumor cells undergo stages of detachment from the primary tumor sites, invasion into vessels (capillary, lymphatics), extravasation into the appropriate secondary site, and establishment of a microenvironment supporting its nourishment and blood supply. Common cancers that metastasize to lung parenchyma include breast, lung, colorectal cancer, uterine leiomyosarcoma, and head/neck squamous cell carcinomas. Cancers that spread to the endobronchial tree of the lungs include colorectal, renal, lung cancer, and lymphomas. Other tumors that can metastasize to the lungs include osteosarcoma and testicular tumors. Other rare cancers described in the literature that metastasize to the lung include adrenal, thyroid, choriocarcinoma, and hypernephroma. Some tumors cannot be identified and classified as cancer of unknown primary (CUP). Around 90% of CUP are adenocarcinomas (90%), with squamous cell carcinomas and undifferentiated carcinomas reportedly less frequent. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Metastasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic bone cancer, also known as secondary bone cancer, is the term used to describe tumors that originate in other tissues and spread (metastasize) to the bone. The rich arterial supply of the bone makes it a common site of metastatic spread. In fact, the skeleton is the third most common site of disease after lung and liver. Primary bone cancer is an uncommon diagnosis. In contrast, a primary bone tumor develops from the uninhibited growth of cancerous bone progenitor cells. The term primary bone cancer implies that the malignancy began within the bone itself and excludes cancers that originate in other tissues and spread (metastasize) to the bone. Secondary bone cancer is a large heterogeneous group that can be responsive to chemotherapy, radiation, or surgery. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IVA (Any T N0 M1a Any G)
retrieved_docs.content: Any size and maybe in multiple locations in the same bone, with no lymph node involvement. Metastatic spread to the lungs (M1a); any grade

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Evaluation -- Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Specific patterns associated with different tumors on CT chest include Diffuse miliary seeding (medullary carcinoma of the thyroid), large singular metastases (choriocarcinoma, melanoma, and hypernephroma), calcification of metastases (osteosarcoma, adenocarcinoma, and secondary to chemo- and radiation therapy), and cavitation of pulmonary metastases (squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck and from the genitourinary tract in women). Most of the hematogenous metastasis appears to happen from the distal pulmonary artery nidus of metastatic tumor cells, resulting in most of the metastatic focus on basal and peripheral segments of the lungs. [11] Chest x-ray patterns recognized with lung metastasis via lymphatic spread include reticular or reticulonodular interstitial markings, thickening of the interlobular septa (Kerley B lines), hilar adenopathy, and pleural disease. High-resolution CT is sensitive to detecting patterns such as thickened core structures in the central portions of the secondary pulmonary lobules. [11] Pleural metastases may appear as nodules or plaque-like formations on plain films and CT scans. Malignant pleural effusions, seen in around 42% of cases, most commonly arise from primary tumors of the lungs, breast, ovaries, and lymphoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Cancer -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Metastasis from lung cancer to bone is frequently symptomatic, and patients may present with bone pain at the site of metastasis in the setting of elevated serum alkaline phosphatase and hypercalcemia. Up to 20% of the patients with non-small cell lung cancer may have bone pain secondary to metastasis on initial presentation; [25] whereas the percentage is as high as 30 to 40% in patients with small-cell lung cancer. [26] Imaging usually reveals osteolytic lesions with vertebral bodies as the most common site of metastasis. Adrenal metastases also occur in lung cancer, but they are rarely symptomatic and are usually seen on staging. However, not all adrenal lesions are malignant lesions, and positron emission tomography (PET) scanning is recommended to differentiate benign from malignant adrenal lesions. [27] Brain metastasis is another common feature of lung cancer in small cell lung cancers (SCLC) and non-small cell lung cancers (NSCLC). In SCLC, brain metastases may be present in as high as 20 to 30% of the patients at diagnosis. [28] Other common sites of metastases in lung cancer include the liver, which is usually only symptomatic in advanced disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoclastoma (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Pulmonary metastases generally are not as aggressive as other solid tumors. However, with reports of fatal cases, surgical resection is the primary treatment. Other options include denosumab therapy, monitoring with chest computed tomography (CT), or whole-lung radiation therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage III (Any G, Any T, M1)
retrieved_docs.content: Any grade, any location, regional, or distant metastatic spread

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Differential Diagnosis -- Malignant Types of Tumors
retrieved_docs.content: Metastases Lymphoma Multiple myeloma

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Stage IIB (G2 T2 M0)
retrieved_docs.content: High grade, extra compartmental, no metastasis

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Bone Cancer -- Staging -- Enneking System
retrieved_docs.content: Refers to the histological grade (G), the extent of the tumor to the anatomical compartments of the body (T), and metastatic spread (M). [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteosarcoma (Osteogenic Sarcoma) -- History and Physical -- Clinical History of Osteogenic Sarcoma
retrieved_docs.content: Around 10% of patients present with pathological fractures due to primary tumors or bony metastases (ie, bone-to-bone spread). [26] Systemic symptoms seen in lymphoma (eg, fever and night sweats) are rare. [5] Respiratory symptoms (eg, frequent cough and hemoptysis) are also uncommon and, when present, indicate extensive lung involvement. Additional symptoms are unusual because metastases to other sites are extremely rare but typically pertain to the involved organ. [5] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoclastoma (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The giant cell tumor of bone (GCTB), also known as an osteoclastoma or a myeloid sarcoma, is a benign local aggressive osteolytic neoplasm that primarily affects skeletally mature young adults. [1] These usually arise in the metaphysis and extend into the epiphysis of long bones. While most GCTB are benign, they rarely metastasize to the lungs. Clinically their behavior can be unpredictable. A histopathological exam is needed to confirm the diagnosis. Imaging of the primary site with plain films and CT is the recommended diagnostic approach. A chest CT or chest X-ray is also recommended to evaluate for metastases to the lungs. [2] Treatment is usually curettage or wide resection, with typically a good prognosis. Rarely GCTB can under malignant transformation. These may be primary or secondary. Primary typically occurs adjacent to the benign GCTB while the secondary occurs at the site of treatment (usually radiation therapy). Malignant GCTB has a poor prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Lung Metastasis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Abnormal lab suggesting metastatic disease includes anemia, hypercalcemia, and electrolyte disturbances (SIADH). A chest X-ray is initial imaging usually performed in both symptomatic patients and patients with known primary tumors. It is cost-effective and readily available. The downside is that small metastasis or miliary distribution is not visible. In 1 study, high kilovolts radiation helps detect pulmonary nodules up to 5 to 10 mm on a chest X-ray. Computed tomography (CT) of the chest is the next option, with helical or multi-planer projection or maximum intensity projection to increase sensitivity. Spiral CT is more sensitive due to a higher metastasis detection rate than other imaging techniques. Up to 72% to 97% nodules and as little as 3 mm nodules are detectable on 5 to 10 mm slices. The sensitivity of CT scans decreases due to high false negatives attributed to unequal respiratory cycles. [10] Positron emission tomography (PET) with fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) is used to detect metastasis elsewhere in the body. PET-CT is used for the precise location of metastasis superimposed on a CT scan.

retrieved_docs.title: Bone Metastasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The skeleton is the third most common site of metastatic disease after lung and liver. Most bone metastases originate from the breast, prostate or lung although kidney and thyroid tumors can metastasize to bone as well. Carcinoma travels to bone via hematogenous spread or direct invasion of bone leading to severe pain and increased risk of pathologic fracture. This review outlines the etiology, epidemiology, clinical presentation, evaluation, and management of bone metastasis and shows the importance of a multidisciplinary approach to patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2437
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of the disease سرطانة العظم. In patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of metastatic lesions indicates that malignant cells have spread to bone from another primary site, while secondary lesions represent additional tumor deposits that develop as a consequence of the primary bone malignancy. Recognizing these lesions is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and the formulation of appropriate treatment strategies for سرطانة العظم.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2556
retrieved_docs.content: Primary malignant neoplasm of the nasal bone and primary malignant neoplasm of the thymus are both examples of primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, representing pathological processes in which cancerous cells originate and grow aggressively at their respective anatomical sites. As primary malignant neoplastic proliferations, each condition reflects a direct, localized malignant transformation of tissue—one affecting the nasal bone and the other affecting the thymus. Recognizing that both entities share the same fundamental nature as pathological processes of primary malignant neoplastic growth aids clinicians in understanding their aggressive behavior, guiding diagnostic evaluation, and informing treatment strategies tailored to the specific organ involved.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 254
retrieved_docs.content: as a pivotal concept that connects a variety of primary tumors (breast, thyroid, skin, bone, thymus, endocrine glands, respiratory and intrathoracic organs) with their secondary sites (bone, ovary, pancreas), and situates specific neoplastic entities within broader classifications of malignant and uncertain‑behavior neoplasms, thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding metastatic disease pathways and their relevance to patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: “metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ” and simultaneously a parent of that same category, illustrating a bidirectional hierarchical relationship within thoracic metastasis. “Metastatic Lesion” and the generic “Secondary Lesion” are recorded as findings for thymic metastasis, and thymic metastasis is further classified as a subtype of “metastatic malignant neoplasm to endocrine gland,” situating it among endocrine‑gland metastases and linking it to the broader group “cancer; other and unspecified primary.” The framework also distinguishes cellular morphology across malignancies: - “Tumor cells, benign” are explicitly noted as not being findings for kidney and renal pelvis cancer, ovarian cancer, other and unspecified primary cancers, malignant neoplasm without specification of site, pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), and bone cancer (سرطانة العظم). - “Tumor cells, malignant” are identified as abnormal cells for all of the same disease entities, confirming malignant

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: connected to the metastasis concept (النقيلة), reinforcing its role as a metastatic disease. Overall, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical network in which a general metastatic cancer node (kanker; metastase) is nested under various primary and secondary sites (skin, bone, respiratory/intrathoracic organs, thymus, endocrine glands). The broader neoplasm categories (unspecified malignant bone/cartilage tumor and neoplasms of uncertain benignity/malignancy) are tied to metastatic potential, emphasizing the clinical importance of recognizing metastatic behavior across diverse tumor types and anatomical locations for accurate diagnosis, staging, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2232
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the clinical evaluation of patients with سرطانة العظم, the identification of secondary lesions indicates disease spread or metastatic involvement, which is essential for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and formulation of appropriate therapeutic strategies. Recognizing secondary lesions therefore plays a critical role in the comprehensive management of bone cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2655
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Secondary Lesion are both recognized as diagnostic findings of سرطانة العظم, indicating the presence of bone cancer. The identification of a malignant cellular infiltrate reflects the invasion of cancerous cells into bone tissue, while a secondary lesion denotes additional disease sites that arise as the primary bone tumor progresses. Recognizing these findings together aids clinicians in confirming the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم, assessing disease extent, and guiding appropriate therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: of primary tumors, including kidney and renal pelvis cancer, ovarian cancer, pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), bone cancer (سرطانة العظم), other and unspecified primary cancers, and malignant neoplasm without a specified site, indicating that this infiltrate is a non‑specific marker of malignant disease. Metastatic disease to specific sites is detailed as follows: - “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes” is a secondary lesion documented as a disease finding, associated with malignant tumor cells and tumor cells of uncertain malignant potential, while benign tumor cells are explicitly excluded. Both “Malignant Cellular Infiltrate” and “Metastatic Lesion” are findings of this supraclavicular metastasis. - “Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus” is a child of “metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ” and simultaneously a parent of that same category, illustrating a bidirectional hierarchical relationship within thoracic metastasis. “Metastatic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 1214
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are clinical findings that can be observed in patients with سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). Both entities may present as Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, indicating that the primary bone malignancy has spread to the supraclavicular lymphatic region. Recognizing Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion as findings of bone cancer and of metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes is important for accurate staging, prognosis assessment, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus is a specific type of metastasis to respiratory and intrathoracic organ, representing the spread of malignant cells to the thymus within the thoracic cavity. This condition is classified as a secondary lesion, indicating that it is a disease finding that arises from a primary malignancy located elsewhere. Recognizing metastatic malignant neoplasm to thymus as a subset of respiratory and intrathoracic organ metastases helps clinicians categorize and manage secondary cancer involvement more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: illustrates that the thymus, an endocrine organ, can serve as a metastatic destination. Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2513
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion are both recognized as pathological findings of سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). The presence of a malignant cellular infiltrate indicates that cancerous cells have invaded the bone tissue, while a metastatic lesion reflects the spread of tumor cells to bone from another primary site. Identifying either of these findings on imaging or histopathology supports the diagnosis of سرطانة العظم and guides clinicians in staging the disease, planning appropriate treatment, and monitoring for disease progression.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 838
retrieved_docs.content: a specific instance of secondary malignancy in defined locations. Other entities listed as members of the non‑hematologic malignant diseases SMQ include سرطان الرئة للخلايا عديمة الرائحة (lung carcinoma with non‑small cells) and the various metastatic and primary head‑and‑neck cancer forms described above, illustrating that the SMQ classification aggregates a wide spectrum of malignant neoplasms, both primary and metastatic, across different anatomical sites and histologic types.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are all recognized as clinical findings of both Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes and سرطانة العظم (bone cancer). In the context of Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, these findings indicate that the disease often presents as a secondary lesion in the supraclavicular region and is characterized by the presence of malignant tumor cells. Likewise, in سرطانة العظم, Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion serve as diagnostic indicators, and Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component of the disease. Together, these relationships underscore that bone cancer can give rise to metastatic malignant neoplasms in supraclavicular lymph nodes, with overlapping pathological features such as malignant cellular infiltrates, metastatic lesions, secondary lesions, and malignant tumor cells, all of which are important for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 748
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate, Metastatic Lesion and Secondary Lesion are all recognized as disease‑related findings for malignant neoplasms when the primary site is not specified, as well as for bone cancer (سرطانة العظم). In the context of a Metastatic malignant neoplasm to supraclavicular lymph nodes, both Malignant Cellular Infiltrate and Metastatic Lesion serve as specific pathological findings, and the metastatic neoplasm itself is classified as a Secondary Lesion. Conversely, Tumor cells, benign are explicitly noted as not being findings of disease for either unspecified malignant neoplasms or bone cancer, underscoring that benign cellular elements are not expected in these malignant conditions. This network of relationships clarifies that malignant cellular infiltrates, metastatic lesions and secondary lesions indicate the presence of malignant disease—including metastatic involvement of supraclavicular lymph nodes—while benign tumor cells are excluded from such diagnostic considerations.
